course_title: Topics in Literature: Major Authors: Shakespeare: Past and Present
subject: ENG
course_description: An introduction to one or more major authors in English literature, with an emphasis on literary merit and its determination, canon formation, literary movements, and reading strategies.
course_notes: "Every writer in the English language, I should imagine, has at some point hated Shakespeare." James Baldwin begins. Why I stopped hating Shakespeare? with a claim that many students can relate to, but in this course, we will confront the Bard's alienating "monstrous achievement" by bringing his work into our current moment. In reading four plays - Titus Andronicus, The Tempest, The Merchant of Venice, and Othello-with Premodern Critical Race Studies scholarship, we will consider the relevance of the early modern to today, exploring questions such as: How was race constructed before "race" became legible in the Enlightenment? How did theater aid in the making of a world shaped by nascent capitalism, race-based slavery, and colonial expansion? How are race, gender, and class legislated in the early Atlantic world? Additionally, we will work into the 21st century with a modern play that confronts Shakespeare's relationship to issues of race, gender, class, and colonization that are integral to situating the early modern in a 2026 classroom. This discussion-based course will help you to develop skills in close reading, research and analysis, and written communication, while also engaging in necessary legal, theoretical, and historical frameworks. It will be of interest to many, including Shakespeare lovers, haters, modern theatre enthusiasts, and pre-law students. By the end of the semester, you will join Baldwin in answering the question of why YOU stopped hating Shakespeare.
